summary: Adversarial bug audit of FileID WinUI 3 C#/XAML app (UI-thread/async/lifecycle/IPC)
logs: App audit: 7 finder dimensions, refute-by-default verify
App audit done: 4 confirmed, 0 uncertain, 1 refuted (of 5 raw)

confirmed.title: PeopleViewModel.RefreshAsync writes IsLoading/ErrorMessage off the UI thread (RPC_E_WRONG_THREAD fast-fail)
confirmed.description: RefreshAsync awaits `await Task.Run(() => LoadClusters(token), token).ConfigureAwait(false)` (line 129). Because of ConfigureAwait(false), the continuation — and therefore the `catch` (line 137: `if (!_disposed) ErrorMessage = ex.Message;`) and the `finally` (line 141: `if (!_disposed) IsLoading = false;`) — resume on a thread-pool thread, NOT the captured UI thread. Both `IsLoading` and `ErrorMessage` raise INotifyPropertyChanged synchronously. PeopleView.xaml binds `ProgressRing IsActive="{x:Bind ViewModel.IsLoading, Mode=OneWay}"` (PeopleView.xaml:252) and the view's OnViewModelPropertyChanged re-raises StatusText (bound via x:Bind, PeopleView.xaml:254). x:Bind writes the DependencyProperty on whatever thread PropertyChanged fires, so this performs a XAML write off the UI thread → COMException 0x8001010E (RPC_E_WRONG_THREAD), unhandled in the off-thread async continuation → native process fast-fail. The author already knew this continuation is off-thread — the Clusters collection write IS marshaled via ApplyOnUi (line 198, `_ui.HasThreadAccess ? Replace : _ui.TryEnqueue`) — but the IsLoading/ErrorMessage setters were left unmarshaled. The sibling LibraryViewModel.RefreshAsync handles this correctly by routing every IsLoading/ErrorMessage write through an OnUi(...) helper (LibraryViewModel.cs:428-432) for exactly this reason.
confirmed.impact: Every People-tab refresh that completes (tab open, post-trash refresh, post-clustering auto-refresh via PeopleView.OnEngineChanged, the catch path on a DB error) raises IsLoading=false (and ErrorMessage on failure) from a thread-pool thread, writing ProgressRing.IsActive / StatusText off the UI thread. That is a native fast-fail with no managed exception — the exact crash class this codebase repeatedly hardened against (V15.2/V15.2.1/V15.4). Manifests as an intermittent, dump-less app death after opening/refreshing the People tab.
confirmed.fix: Marshal the IsLoading/ErrorMessage writes to the captured dispatcher, matching LibraryViewModel. Add an `OnUi(Action)` helper (`if (_ui.HasThreadAccess) action(); else _ui.TryEnqueue(() => { if (!_disposed) action(); });`) and wrap the prologue, the catch's `ErrorMessage = ex.Message`, and the finally's `IsLoading = false` in it. (The prologue `IsLoading = true; ErrorMessage = null;` is currently on the UI thread, but routing it through OnUi too keeps it correct if a future caller invokes RefreshAsync off-thread.)

verdict.reasoning:
I traced the full chain and confirmed the bug is real and reachable.

THREAD CONTEXT: All three callers enter PeopleViewModel.RefreshAsync on the UI thread — OnLoadedAsync (PeopleView.xaml.cs:72, a Loaded handler), the OnEngineClientChanged post-clustering refresh dispatched via DispatcherQueue.TryEnqueue (xaml.cs:61-64), and OnToggleHiddenUnknowns (xaml.cs:150). WinUI 3 installs a DispatcherQueueSynchronizationContext on this thread, so absent ConfigureAwait(false) the continuation would resume on the UI thread.

THE HAZARD: RefreshAsync line 129 does `await Task.Run(() => LoadClusters(token), token).ConfigureAwait(false)`. Task.Run runs LoadClusters on a thread-pool thread, and ConfigureAwait(false) means the continuation — including the catch (line 137: `if (!_disposed) ErrorMessage = ex.Message;`) and the finally (line 141: `if (!_disposed) IsLoading = false;`) — resumes on a thread-pool thread, NOT the UI thread.

REACHABLE XAML WRITE: IsLoading's setter (line 98-104) flips true→false (prologue set it true on the UI thread), so the `if (_isLoading == value) return` guard does NOT short-circuit; it raises PropertyChanged synchronously via OnPropertyChanged (line 300-301, no marshaling). PeopleView.xaml line 252 binds `ProgressRing IsActive="{x:Bind ViewModel.IsLoading, Mode=OneWay}"` directly to the VM property — so x:Bind writes the IsActive DependencyProperty on the firing (pool) thread. Additionally the view's OnViewModelPropertyChanged (xaml.cs:155-160) re-raises StatusText/FooterVisibility with NO DispatcherQueue marshaling and NO thread guard; StatusText is x:Bind-bound (xaml.cs:233, xaml line 254). Both are off-UI-thread XAML DependencyProperty writes → COMException 0x8001010E (RPC_E_WRONG_THREAD), unhandled in the off-thread async continuation → native fast-fail. The catch path (DB error) additionally writes ErrorMessage off-thread.

AUTHOR INTENT CONFIRMS IT: The sibling LibraryViewModel.RefreshAsync routes the IDENTICAL catch/finally writes through an OnUi(...) helper (LibraryViewModel.cs:406-407), whose own comment (lines 422-427) names this exact hazard verbatim: 'raising IsLoading/ErrorMessage PropertyChanged there would drive x:Bind writes (ProgressRing.IsActive, StatusText) off the captured UI thread — a native fast-fail.' PeopleViewModel was clearly written before this fix was back-ported, or the back-port missed it: its ApplyOnUi (line 198-208) marshals the Clusters write but the IsLoading/ErrorMessage setters were left unmarshaled. This is precisely the V15.2/V15.2.1/V15.4 crash class the codebase repeatedly hardened against.

I tried to refute via: (a) a value-equality short-circuit in the setter — refuted, value genuinely changes true→false; (b) marshaling inside OnPropertyChanged — refuted, line 300 invokes directly; (c) marshaling inside the view handler — refuted, line 155-160 has none; (d) callers entering off-thread already (which would make ConfigureAwait(false) a no-op for the bug) — refuted, all enter on the UI thread. No guard exists anywhere in the chain.
verdict.fix_assessment: The proposed fix is correct and complete. It mirrors the established, in-tree LibraryViewModel pattern exactly: add an OnUi(Action) helper that no-ops when _ui.HasThreadAccess and otherwise _ui.TryEnqueue's the action with an inner _disposed re-check, then wrap the catch's `ErrorMessage = ex.Message` and the finally's `IsLoading = false` in it. Wrapping the prologue (`IsLoading = true; ErrorMessage = null;`) is correctly described as optional-but-defensive (it runs pre-await on the UI thread today). The inner `if (!_disposed)` re-check inside the enqueued lambda is important (a Dispose can land between enqueue and execution) and the proposed helper includes it, matching LibraryViewModel.OnUi line 431. The only behavioral nuance — that routing IsLoading=false through TryEnqueue makes the write asynchronous — is benign for a 'stop loading' indicator.

confirmed.title: CleanupViewModel.RefreshAsync writes IsLoading/ErrorMessage off the UI thread (RPC_E_WRONG_THREAD fast-fail)
confirmed.description: Identical shape to the PeopleViewModel issue. RefreshAsync awaits `await Task.Run(() => Load(token), token).ConfigureAwait(false)` (line 75). The catch arms (lines 83-85: `ErrorMessage = SqliteErrorTranslator.Humanize(ex)` / `ErrorMessage = ex.Message`) and the `finally` (line 86: `if (!_disposed) IsLoading = false;`) therefore run on a thread-pool thread after ConfigureAwait(false). CleanupView.xaml binds `ProgressRing IsActive="{x:Bind ViewModel.IsLoading, Mode=OneWay}"` (CleanupView.xaml:222) and `StatusText` / `FooterVisibility` (re-raised by OnViewModelPropertyChanged from the VM's PropertyChanged) which read ViewModel.IsLoading/ErrorMessage and are x:Bind targets (CleanupView.xaml:225-226). Setting IsLoading/ErrorMessage off-thread writes those DependencyProperties off the UI thread → RPC_E_WRONG_THREAD native fast-fail. As in People, the Groups collection write is correctly marshaled via ApplyOnUi (line 195-198) but the IsLoading/ErrorMessage setters are not.
confirmed.impact: Every Cleanup-tab refresh completion (OnLoaded, OnRefreshClicked, the post-trash `await ViewModel.RefreshAsync(...)` at CleanupView lines 405/568, and the debounced TryEnqueue refresh) flips IsLoading=false off the UI thread; a SqliteException/IOException path additionally writes ErrorMessage off-thread. Dump-less app death when viewing/refreshing the Cleanup tab, especially after trashing duplicates.
confirmed.fix: Add the same OnUi(Action) marshaling helper (CleanupViewModel already holds `_ui`) and route the IsLoading=true/ErrorMessage=null prologue, the four catch-arm ErrorMessage assignments, and the finally's IsLoading=false through it — mirroring LibraryViewModel.RefreshAsync.

verdict.reasoning:
Independently traced and confirmed. CleanupViewModel.RefreshAsync (CleanupViewModel.cs:63-87) does `var groups = await Task.Run(() => Load(token), token).ConfigureAwait(false);` (line 75). Because of ConfigureAwait(false), the continuation — the four catch arms (lines 83-85: ErrorMessage = ...) and especially the finally (line 86: IsLoading = false), which runs on EVERY completion including the success path — resumes on a thread-pool thread, not the UI thread. Task.Run completes on the pool and ConfigureAwait(false) suppresses the SynchronizationContext recapture, so there is no marshal back.

The setters at lines 54/60 raise OnPropertyChanged synchronously on that pool thread. IsLoading is bound directly: ProgressRing IsActive="{x:Bind ViewModel.IsLoading, Mode=OneWay}" (CleanupView.xaml:222). I confirmed in the generated binding code (CleanupView.g.cs) that PropertyChanged_ViewModel for "IsLoading" calls Update_ViewModel_IsLoading → XamlBindingSetters.Set_Microsoft_UI_Xaml_Controls_ProgressRing_IsActive(this.obj3, obj) with NO thread marshaling — a raw DependencyProperty SetValue. Setting a DependencyProperty from a non-UI thread is an RPC_E_WRONG_THREAD native fast-fail (the exact V15.2/V15.2.1/V15.4 class the project guards against). Additionally, OnViewModelPropertyChanged (CleanupView.xaml.cs:107) re-raises the view's StatusText/FooterVisibility synchronously on the same pool thread, and those are x:Bind OneWay targets too (Set_...TextBlock_Text / Set_...UIElement_Visibility), compounding the off-thread DependencyProperty writes.

Reachable on every Cleanup-tab refresh completion: OnLoadedAsync (tab entry, line 103), OnRefreshClicked (line 299), the debounced RequestCleanupRefresh during a scan (~1 Hz, line 94), and the two post-trash refreshes (lines 405, 568).

The fix is corroborated by the sibling LibraryViewModel.RefreshAsync (lines 266-328), which is structurally identical (same linked-token prologue, same _store SQLite offload via Task.Run + ConfigureAwait(false)) but DELIBERATELY routes the IsLoading=true/ErrorMessage=null prologue (line 276), every catch arm (line 322), and the finally (line 326) through an OnUi(Action) helper (lines 428-432: `if (_ui.HasThreadAccess) action(); else _ui.TryEnqueue(...)`), with an explicit comment (lines 422-428) stating this exact hazard. CleanupViewModel has _ui and ApplyOnUi but no OnUi helper (confirmed by grep) and writes IsLoading/ErrorMessage raw.

One correction to the finding's description: the prologue (IsLoading=true/ErrorMessage=null at lines 73-74) is NOT off-thread for the current call sites — it executes synchronously before the await, and all callers invoke RefreshAsync on the UI thread. So the prologue is incidentally safe today; the genuine off-thread writes are the four catch arms and the finally. This does not change severity — the finally fires on the success path of every refresh, so the crash is reached without needing any exception.
verdict.fix_assessment: The proposed fix is correct and mirrors the established LibraryViewModel pattern. Add an OnUi(Action) helper to CleanupViewModel (it already holds _ui) and route the catch-arm ErrorMessage assignments and the finally's IsLoading=false through it. The prologue (IsLoading=true/ErrorMessage=null) does not strictly need wrapping for today's UI-thread call sites, but wrapping it as LibraryViewModel does is harmless and defends against a future non-UI-thread caller, so following the fix verbatim is fine.

confirmed.title: RestartAsync races OnProcessExited's Cleanup → freshly-spawned engine is torn down, State stuck Starting, all IPC fails
confirmed.description:
RestartAsync (Settings -> 'Restart engine', and the post-cuDNN-install restart) does: await StopAndWaitForExitAsync(10s).ConfigureAwait(false); StartAsync(); WaitForReadyAsync(30s). Two problems compound:
1. StopAndWaitForExitAsync (EngineClient.Commands.cs:246-269) returns as soon as `_process.HasExited` is observed by its 100 ms poll loop. The old process's Process.Exited event fires on a thread-pool thread and only QUEUES OnProcessExited's body via `_ui.TryEnqueue` (EngineClient.cs:726). That queued Cleanup() has almost certainly NOT run yet when StopAndWaitForExitAsync returns.
2. Because RestartAsync awaits with ConfigureAwait(false), the continuation that calls StartAsync() runs on a thread-pool thread, NOT the UI thread. So StartAsync() spawns the NEW process and assigns the fields `_process`, `_readCts`, `_stdin`, `_stdoutLoop` (EngineClient.cs:530-536) with NO lock, concurrently with the UI-thread Cleanup() for the OLD process.
When the queued OnProcessExited->Cleanup() (EngineClient.cs:729 / 792-815) then runs AFTER StartAsync has installed the new fields, it operates on the NEW process's state: it cancels the new `_readCts` (killing the new stdout/stderr pumps), disposes the new `_stdin`, removes Exited handler from the new process, and sets `_process = null`. The new engine process is left alive but with its IPC pumps dead and `_stdin = null`. State never advances to Ready (no ReadyEvent is ever read), so WaitForReadyAsync(30s) times out, and every subsequent SendCommandAsync fails ('Engine not running' / state != Ready). No Exited event fires for the new process (it's still alive), so the crash-respawn path never recovers it. Contrast the normal crash path (EngineClient.cs:724-789), which runs Cleanup() synchronously FIRST and only schedules the respawn StartAsync 1s+ later on the UI thread under the _isStarting CAS — so it has no such race.
confirmed.impact: User clicks Settings 'Restart engine' (or the engine auto-restarts after a GPU/cuDNN pack install): with realistic timing the new engine's IPC loops are immediately cancelled and _stdin nulled. The engine card shows 'Restarting…' / Starting forever (WaitForReadyAsync times out after 30 s), scans and every other command fail, and there is no automatic recovery for the rest of the session — the user must kill and relaunch the app. Because the field writes are an unsynchronized cross-thread data race, behavior is timing-dependent and intermittent (matches the 'unverified-on-hardware, intermittent restart wedge' risk profile).
confirmed.fix: Serialize spawn vs. teardown. Options: (a) make OnProcessExited ignore exits for a process that is no longer the current `_process` (capture `sender as Process` and early-return if `!ReferenceEquals(sender, _process)`), and have Cleanup only act on the matching process; and/or (b) in RestartAsync, after StopAndWaitForExitAsync, marshal the StartAsync call onto the UI thread (drop ConfigureAwait(false) for that step, or _ui.TryEnqueue it) so it cannot run concurrently with the UI-thread Cleanup, AND ensure the old process's Cleanup has drained (e.g. await the queued exit handler) before spawning. The cleanest fix is to tag each spawn with a generation/epoch and have Cleanup/OnProcessExited no-op when the epoch no longer matches.

verdict.reasoning:
I traced the full chain and the race is real and reachable.

THREADING FACTS (verified):
- RestartAsync (EngineClient.Commands.cs:279-298) awaits all three steps with ConfigureAwait(false), so after StopAndWaitForExitAsync the continuation calling StartAsync runs on a thread-pool thread, NOT the UI thread.
- StopAndWaitForExitAsync (Commands.cs:258-268) returns as soon as its 100ms poll sees _process.HasExited==true. This is independent of the Process.Exited event.
- OnProcessExited (EngineClient.cs:724-790) only QUEUES its body via _ui.TryEnqueue; the actual Cleanup() runs later on the UI thread.
- Cleanup() (EngineClient.cs:792-815) operates BLINDLY on the live instance fields _readCts/_stdin/_process. I grepped for any sender check or epoch/generation guard (ReferenceEquals/sender as Process/_generation/_epoch) — NONE exist. Confirmed.
- StartAsync field installs (EngineClient.cs:530-540: _process, _stdin, _readCts, _stdoutLoop, _stderrLoop, += OnProcessExited) are done with NO lock for _process/_readCts (only Cleanup takes _writeLock, and only around _stdin). So spawn and teardown are not serialized.

THE RACE:
The UI-queued OnProcessExited->Cleanup() for the OLD process can land AFTER the thread-pool StartAsync has installed the NEW process's fields. This ordering is realistic because StopAndWaitForExitAsync returns on a HasExited poll (no Exited-event/dispatch dependency), the Exited event itself fires asynchronously and adds a _ui.TryEnqueue hop, while StartAsync's synchronous field installs on the threadpool have no dispatch hop. When Cleanup then runs it: cancels the NEW _readCts (killing the new StdoutLoopAsync, which awaits on ct at EngineClient.cs:609), disposes/nulls the NEW _stdin, removes the Exited handler from the NEW process, and sets _process=null.

CONSEQUENCE (verified end-to-end):
State only reaches Ready when StdoutLoopAsync reads a ReadyEvent and sets State=LifecycleState.Ready (EngineClient.cs:937/951). With the new stdout loop cancelled, no ReadyEvent is ever read, so WaitForReadyAsync(30s) (Commands.cs:296) times out. The new engine is left ALIVE but with dead IPC pumps and _stdin=null, so every subsequent SendCommandAsync fails. No new Exited fires (process is alive), so the crash-respawn path never recovers it for the rest of the session. Additionally, _expectingExit was set to 1 by ShutdownAsync (Commands.cs:228), so even if OnProcessExited wins, it hits the early-return at EngineClient.cs:742-747 (State=Crashed, no respawn) — but Cleanup() at line 729 has already run unconditionally and torn down the new process. (There is also a secondary 3-way data race on _expectingExit because StartAsync line 407 clears it; in the variant where OnProcessExited sees 0 it schedules yet another StartAsync, compounding the double-spawn — still broken.)

This is timing-dependent/intermittent, consistent with the unverified-on-hardware restart-wedge risk profile. The contrast the finding draws with the normal crash path is correct: that path runs Cleanup() synchronously first and only schedules respawn 1s+ later on the UI thread, so it has no such race. Severity high stands: user-triggered Settings 'Restart engine' (two call sites: SettingsView.xaml.cs:1011 and :1039) and the post-cuDNN-install restart can wedge the engine for the whole session with no automatic recovery.
verdict.fix_assessment: The finding's preferred fix (option c: tag each spawn with a generation/epoch and have Cleanup/OnProcessExited no-op when the epoch no longer matches) is correct and the cleanest. Options (a) and (b) alone are weaker: (a) capturing `sender as Process` and early-returning when it is not the current _process closes the OnProcessExited-vs-new-process hole, but does NOT by itself serialize StartAsync's unlocked field writes (530-540) against Cleanup's field writes — so it must be combined with a lock; (b) marshalling StartAsync back onto the UI thread plus draining the old exit handler is sufficient but more fragile (relies on dispatcher ordering and an extra await of the queued handler).
verdict.corrected_fix:
Use an epoch/generation guard AND serialize the spawn/teardown field swap under one lock (not just _writeLock, which currently covers only _stdin):

1. Add `private int _spawnGen;` to EngineClient. In StartAsync, before installing the new process fields, capture `int gen = Interlocked.Increment(ref _spawnGen);` and pass `gen` into the closures: `p.Exited += (s, e) => OnProcessExited(s, e, gen);` and `Task.Run(() => StdoutLoopAsync(p.StandardOutput, ct), ct)` etc. Do the field installs (530-540) under a shared lock (introduce `_lifecycleLock`).

2. Change OnProcessExited to take the captured gen and, inside the _ui.TryEnqueue body (or before it), early-return if `gen != Volatile.Read(ref _spawnGen)` — an exit for a superseded process must not run Cleanup or the respawn/Crashed transition.

3. Change Cleanup to take/check the same gen (or have it only run for the current generation) and take `_lifecycleLock` so it cannot interleave with StartAsync's field installs. Cleanup must no-op if its captured gen != current _spawnGen.

4. In RestartAsync, additionally avoid the cross-thread spawn-vs-teardown overlap: after StopAndWaitForExitAsync, either drop ConfigureAwait(false) for the StartAsync step / _ui.TryEnqueue it so it runs on the UI thread (where the queued Cleanup also runs, serializing them), and/or await drainage of the old exit handler before spawning. The epoch guard makes this race benign even if the marshalling is imperfect, so do BOTH for defence in depth.

Net effect: a late Cleanup/OnProcessExited for the OLD engine sees a stale gen and no-ops, leaving the freshly-spawned engine's _readCts/_stdin/_process and stdout loop intact, so ReadyEvent is read and State reaches Ready.

confirmed.title: Single-file DeepAnalyzeFileAsync waiter resolves on an unrelated DeepAnalyzeComplete (batch or other single-file), surfacing a wrong/false result
confirmed.description: DeepAnalyzeFileAsync resets DeepAnalyzeComplete = null, subscribes a handler that completes on the FIRST DeepAnalyzeComplete becoming non-null, then sends deepAnalyzeFile and waits 5 min. There is no per-request correlation: the DeepAnalyzeComplete observable property is shared by the single-file path AND the batch 'Analyze All' path (DeepAnalyzeAllAsync -> DeepAnalyzeCompleteEvent, EngineClient.cs:1113-1120). These callsites are genuinely concurrently reachable: DeepAnalyzeView.OnAnalyzeAllClicked (DeepAnalyzeView.xaml.cs:721) only disables its own AnalyzeAllButton, while FilePreviewSheet.OnAnalyzeClicked (FilePreviewSheet.xaml.cs:844-850) and DeepAnalyzeView.OnAnalyzeSelectedClicked/OnAnalyzeCurrentClicked (DeepAnalyzeView.xaml.cs:753-779) can fire a single-file deepAnalyzeFile in parallel. Whichever DeepAnalyzeComplete lands first (e.g. the long batch's terminal event, or the other file's) satisfies the single-file waiter, which then reads result.Cancelled/result.Failed from the WRONG run.
confirmed.impact: Not a hang (the wait is bounded and always resolves), but the single-file Deep Analyze can show a spurious LastWarning ('Deep Analyze couldn't process this file…') or a false success based on an unrelated run's result. Also, the single-file path's `DeepAnalyzeComplete = null` reset transiently clears the batch run's terminal-state tracking. Edge-case, requires two Deep Analyze actions overlapping.
confirmed.fix: Correlate the reply: thread a request id / file id through deepAnalyzeFile and the DeepAnalyzeComplete event and only resolve the waiter when the ids match, or serialize single-file analyze behind the same in-flight gate used for 'Analyze All' so the two cannot overlap.

verdict.reasoning:
The finding identifies a real, reachable bug but its stated mechanism is partly wrong; I confirmed the outcome via a path it did not trace.

CONFIRMED structural premise: DeepAnalyzeFileAsync (EngineClient.Commands.cs:406-452) resets the shared DeepAnalyzeComplete property to null (line 418), subscribes a handler that resolves on the FIRST non-null DeepAnalyzeComplete (lines 410-417), sends deepAnalyzeFile, and waits up to 5 min. There is NO per-request correlation: the IPC DeepAnalyzeComplete event carries no file_id/request_id (engine commands/deep_analyze.rs:140-149, 60-68), and the SAME DeepAnalyzeComplete property is fed by both the single-file path and the batch DeepAnalyzeCompleteEvent arm (EngineClient.cs:1113-1120).

REFUTED reachability sub-claim: the finding says the two paths are "genuinely concurrently reachable" and that whichever run's terminal event lands first satisfies the single-file waiter because both RUN in parallel. That is wrong. The engine serializes all three deep-analyze variants behind a shared atomic gate deep_analyze_active (main.rs:692-698 DeepAnalyzeFile, 714-720 DeepAnalyzeFolder, 736-742 DeepAnalyzeAll). A second deep-analyze command while one is in flight fails compare_exchange and is rejected via emit_deep_analyze_busy -> Error("deep_analyze_already_running") (main.rs:456-464). It emits NO DeepAnalyzeComplete, so two runs never execute concurrently.

CONFIRMED outcome via the correct path (engine busy-rejection, not concurrent execution): With a batch "Analyze All" already running (deep_analyze_active=true), a single-file analyze is still triggerable — the app does NOT gate the single-file buttons on batch state (DeepAnalyzeView SyncSelectionButtons:115-127 enables AnalyzeSelected/AnalyzeCurrent purely on selection; FilePreviewSheet.OnAnalyzeClicked:844-860 only disables its own AnalyzeButton). DeepAnalyzeFileAsync subscribes its uncorrelated waiter and sends deepAnalyzeFile; the engine busy-rejects it (Error -> LastError, no terminal event for this file). The waiter STAYS subscribed and is later satisfied by the BATCH's terminal DeepAnalyzeComplete. It then reads Cancelled/Failed from the batch run: if the batch had Failed>0 it raises a spurious LastWarning "Deep Analyze couldn't process this file…" (Commands.cs:431-438) attributed to the single file; if the batch was clean it reports a false silent success — in both cases the single file was never analyzed. If the batch outlasts the 5-min DeepAnalyzeFileTimeout, the waiter instead throws TimeoutException -> false "hasn't responded in 5 minutes" warning (Commands.cs:440-447). LastWarning is user-visible via SidebarProcessingControl (SidebarProcessingControl.xaml.cs:339). The DeepAnalyzeComplete=null reset at line 418 is benign here because the batch already nulled it at start (EngineClient.cs:1099) and only sets it non-null at completion.

So: same end-user symptom the finding claims (single-file waiter resolves on the unrelated batch's terminal event, surfacing a wrong/false result), reachable with two overlapping user actions, but via busy-rejection rather than parallel execution.
verdict.fix_assessment: The finding's proposed fix is directionally correct but incomplete given the actual (busy-rejection) mechanism. Serializing the single-file path behind the same in-flight gate as "Analyze All" would prevent the overlap and is the simplest robust option. Correlating by id alone is necessary but NOT sufficient: even with id correlation, a single-file request that the engine busy-rejects produces NO terminal DeepAnalyzeComplete for that id, so an id-correlated waiter would never resolve on a foreign event but would instead always TIMEOUT after 5 minutes (still a false "hasn't responded" warning and a 5-min stuck card). So the fix must ALSO treat Error(deep_analyze_already_running) as a terminal signal for the in-flight single-file waiter.
verdict.corrected_fix:
Two coordinated changes:
1) Correlation: thread a request/file id from DeepAnalyzeFileCommand through the engine's single-file DeepAnalyzeComplete event (commands/deep_analyze.rs:140-149, 60-68, 161-170) and only resolve the DeepAnalyzeFileAsync waiter when ids match (Commands.cs:410-417). This stops the waiter from latching onto the batch's terminal event.
2) Terminal busy/error handling: have DeepAnalyzeFileAsync also subscribe to LastError and immediately resolve/abort its waiter (with the busy message surfaced once) when an Error with kind "deep_analyze_already_running" (or a matching-id deep_analyze_failed) lands — otherwise an id-correlated waiter for a busy-rejected request just sits until the 5-min timeout and emits a false "no response" warning.

Simpler alternative that needs no IPC change: gate the single-file analyze entry points behind the same client-side in-flight check used for batch (i.e., refuse/queue a single-file analyze while DeepAnalyzeStarting/DeepAnalyzeProgress indicates a run is active, mirroring the engine's deep_analyze_active gate), so the single-file waiter is never created while a batch is running and cannot resolve on a foreign terminal event.
